mnemonics.title: Disk Scheduling Algorithms
mnemonics.content: FSSCC: FCFS → SSTF → SCAN → C-SCAN → LOOK → C-LOOK (progressive optimization)

mnemonics.title: SCAN vs C-SCAN
mnemonics.content: SCAN: Serves on the way UP and DOWN | C-SCAN: Serves only on the way UP, walks down empty (like escalator)

mnemonics.title: Access Time Components
mnemonics.content: SAT = Seek Time + Rotational Latency + Transfer Time (SART for short)

acronyms.usage: Services requests in arrival order - simple but not optimized
Services closest request to head - efficient but may starve distant requests
Moves end-to-end servicing requests - no starvation, good performance
One-way sweep providing uniform wait times
Like SCAN but stops at last request - more efficient than SCAN
Like C-SCAN but stops at last request - efficient circular access

visualTips.visualization: Imagine an elevator in a building that stops at every floor where someone is waiting, going from bottom to top, then reversing to serve everyone on the way down.
Think of a circular carousel or merry-go-round - it serves everyone in order as it passes, then returns to the starting point without stopping.
Think of picking the nearest person needing help from your current location - fastest to help one person, but faraway people may wait forever.
Visualize the disk head as a needle on a vinyl record, moving to different tracks - shorter movements = less time = better performance.

analogies.analogy: Like a queue at a ticket counter - first person in line gets served first, regardless of how close they are to the exit.
Like a taxi driver picking up passengers - always picks up whoever is closest to the taxi, which is efficient but distant customers wait long.
Like an elevator in a building - goes from ground to top floor serving everyone on the way, then goes back down serving everyone.
Like a security checkpoint at an airport - everyone moves forward in one direction through the scanner, then returns to the start of the line.
If you always serve the closest customer, someone standing far away may never get served if new close customers keep appearing.
Like a vinyl record player needle - moving between inner tracks (center) and outer tracks (edge) takes time, so minimizing jumps saves music playback time.
